verses.text: On the very day their enemies hoped to crush them, the tables are turned, and no one can stand against the Jews, for fear of them and of the now-powerful Mordecai falls on all. The intended day of death becomes a day of triumph. God reverses the doom His people dreaded.

On the very day their enemies hoped to crush them, the tables are turned, and no one can stand against the Jews, for fear of them and of the now-powerful Mordecai falls on all. The intended day of death becomes a day of triumph. God reverses the doom His people dreaded.

On the very day their enemies hoped to crush them, the tables are turned, and no one can stand against the Jews, for fear of them and of the now-powerful Mordecai falls on all. The intended day of death becomes a day of triumph. God reverses the doom His people dreaded.

On the very day their enemies hoped to crush them, the tables are turned, and no one can stand against the Jews, for fear of them and of the now-powerful Mordecai falls on all. The intended day of death becomes a day of triumph. God reverses the doom His people dreaded.

The Jews strike down those who hated them, gaining the victory their enemies had plotted to win. The aggressors reap what they devised. Those who set themselves against God's people find the sword turned back upon their own hand.

The Jews strike down those who hated them, gaining the victory their enemies had plotted to win. The aggressors reap what they devised. Those who set themselves against God's people find the sword turned back upon their own hand.

The Jews strike down those who hated them, gaining the victory their enemies had plotted to win. The aggressors reap what they devised. Those who set themselves against God's people find the sword turned back upon their own hand.

Haman's ten sons are slain, yet the Jews lay no hand on the spoil. They defend their lives but seek no plunder. Their restraint shows the war was for survival, not greed, and clears their cause before God and man.

Haman's ten sons are slain, yet the Jews lay no hand on the spoil. They defend their lives but seek no plunder. Their restraint shows the war was for survival, not greed, and clears their cause before God and man.

Haman's ten sons are slain, yet the Jews lay no hand on the spoil. They defend their lives but seek no plunder. Their restraint shows the war was for survival, not greed, and clears their cause before God and man.
Told the numbers slain in Shushan, the king asks Esther what more she desires. He stands ready to grant still more. God can incline the powerful to keep extending favour to His people in their need.
Told the numbers slain in Shushan, the king asks Esther what more she desires. He stands ready to grant still more. God can incline the powerful to keep extending favour to His people in their need.
Esther asks one more day and that Haman's sons be hung up, and it is done. The work of judgment is made thorough. Evil deeply rooted must be wholly uprooted, lest it spring up again.
Esther asks one more day and that Haman's sons be hung up, and it is done. The work of judgment is made thorough. Evil deeply rooted must be wholly uprooted, lest it spring up again.
Esther asks one more day and that Haman's sons be hung up, and it is done. The work of judgment is made thorough. Evil deeply rooted must be wholly uprooted, lest it spring up again.

The Jews throughout the provinces defend themselves, then rest and feast on the next day. Deliverance gives way to thanksgiving. When God has saved, His people turn from the battle to rejoicing in His goodness.

The Jews throughout the provinces defend themselves, then rest and feast on the next day. Deliverance gives way to thanksgiving. When God has saved, His people turn from the battle to rejoicing in His goodness.

Because Shushan fought an extra day and rested later, the country Jews keep the fourteenth and the city the fifteenth as days of gladness. Their joy is marked and shared. Mercies received deserve a remembered day of praise.

Because Shushan fought an extra day and rested later, the country Jews keep the fourteenth and the city the fifteenth as days of gladness. Their joy is marked and shared. Mercies received deserve a remembered day of praise.

Mordecai writes to establish these as yearly days of feasting, gifts to one another, and gifts to the poor — the days their sorrow turned to joy. Remembrance is bound to generosity. Gratitude for deliverance overflows in giving, especially to those in need.

Mordecai writes to establish these as yearly days of feasting, gifts to one another, and gifts to the poor — the days their sorrow turned to joy. Remembrance is bound to generosity. Gratitude for deliverance overflows in giving, especially to those in need.

Mordecai writes to establish these as yearly days of feasting, gifts to one another, and gifts to the poor — the days their sorrow turned to joy. Remembrance is bound to generosity. Gratitude for deliverance overflows in giving, especially to those in need.

The Jews confirm the custom, recalling how Haman's plot and his Pur recoiled upon his own head. The story is kept to teach the lesson. God's reversals are remembered so that each generation sees how He defends His own.

The Jews confirm the custom, recalling how Haman's plot and his Pur recoiled upon his own head. The story is kept to teach the lesson. God's reversals are remembered so that each generation sees how He defends His own.

The Jews confirm the custom, recalling how Haman's plot and his Pur recoiled upon his own head. The story is kept to teach the lesson. God's reversals are remembered so that each generation sees how He defends His own.

These days are called Purim, after the lot, to be kept by every family in every generation, never to fail among the Jews. A lasting feast is founded. The deliverances of God are worth a memorial that outlives those who first received them.

These days are called Purim, after the lot, to be kept by every family in every generation, never to fail among the Jews. A lasting feast is founded. The deliverances of God are worth a memorial that outlives those who first received them.

These days are called Purim, after the lot, to be kept by every family in every generation, never to fail among the Jews. A lasting feast is founded. The deliverances of God are worth a memorial that outlives those who first received them.

Esther and Mordecai confirm Purim with letters of peace and truth, joining fasting with their gladness, and it is written in the record. The feast is doubly established. What God has done is set down in writing, that His faithfulness be never lost.
